Daily DAP Accessibility Report — 2026-03-10#

Run ID: run-2026-03-10-80230b0f4e · Status: success · Source data: 2026-03-08 · Generated: 2026-03-10T09:31:39.675Z

About These Reports#

The Digital Analytics Program (DAP) is a U.S. government analytics service that collects website traffic data across participating federal agencies. DAP tracks page views, visitor counts, and usage patterns for hundreds of government websites, providing transparency into how the public engages with federal digital services.

This report measures the quality and accessibility of the top 100 most-visited U.S. government URLs as reported by DAP. Each day, Lighthouse scans are run against these URLs to measure:

  • Performance - How fast pages load for users (scores 0-100, higher is better)
  • Accessibility - How well pages work for users with disabilities, following WCAG guidelines (scores 0-100, higher is better)
  • Best Practices - Whether pages follow modern web development standards (scores 0-100, higher is better)
  • SEO - How well pages are optimized for search engines (scores 0-100, higher is better)

Accessibility findings come from axe-core, the industry-standard accessibility testing engine embedded in Lighthouse. The axe findings surface specific WCAG violations such as missing alternative text, insufficient color contrast, and missing form labels that make government websites harder to use for people with disabilities.

Traffic data reflects daily visitor counts from DAP. URLs are ranked by page load count, ensuring the most-used government pages are prioritized for quality measurement.

Accessibility Trend Narrative#

Over the past 12 days of data (2026-02-26 to 2026-03-09), government website accessibility scores have improved (+23.98 points, from 68 to 91.98). Performance scores have improved (+10.02 points, from 38 to 48.02).

Today's aggregate accessibility score of 91.66 reflects the mean Lighthouse accessibility score across 100 successfully scanned government URLs. A score above 90 indicates generally strong compliance with WCAG automated checks, though manual testing is always recommended to fully assess accessibility.

Human impact narratives for top issues

The following narratives describe the real-world impact of each accessibility barrier on people with disabilities. These barriers are not abstract technical failures; they prevent citizens from independently accessing government services.

target-size: Digital Motor Access

Small touch targets act as a digital gatekeeper, excluding individuals with tremors, arthritis, or limited dexterity from accessing essential services independently. These technical failures transform a routine interaction into a source of failure, stripping away the autonomy of citizens who require a frictionless, accessible interface to participate in digital life. The approximately 58 million Americans with ambulatory or self-care disabilities are disproportionately impacted by inadequate touch target sizing on government mobile websites.

Affected groups:

  • People with Parkinson's disease, arthritis, or hand tremors
  • Older adults with reduced fine motor control
  • People with motor disabilities using alternative pointing devices
  • People in situational impairment contexts (e.g., commuting, holding a child)

heading-order: Document Structure Navigation

Screen reader users navigate complex government websites primarily through heading structure, using headings as a table of contents to jump between sections. Skipped heading levels break the logical document outline, causing confusion about the hierarchy of information and forcing users to re-read sections to understand the relationship between topics, adding significant time and effort to information-gathering tasks.

Affected groups:

  • People who are blind using screen readers
  • People who are deaf and rely on visual-to-text tools
  • People with motor disabilities using keyboard navigation

color-contrast: Visual Information Access

Low contrast text is one of the most pervasive barriers on government websites, rendering critical information invisible to the approximately 26 million Americans with low vision or color blindness. When agency announcements, form instructions, error messages, or legal notices are displayed in insufficient contrast, affected citizens are denied equal access to the information they need to exercise their rights and access public services.

Affected groups:

  • People with low vision including age-related vision loss
  • People who are color blind (approximately 8% of men, 0.5% of women)

landmark-one-main: Primary Content Identification

Without a main landmark, screen reader users lose their most efficient way to skip to the primary content of a government page. Every page visit requires tabbing through the entire navigation structure to reach the actual content, a significant time and effort burden that accumulates across repeated visits to government websites for citizens managing ongoing benefit cases or legal matters.

Affected groups:

  • People who are blind using screen readers
  • People who are deaf and rely on visual-to-text tools
  • People with motor disabilities using keyboard navigation

label-content-name-mismatch: Voice Control Activation

When the accessible name of an element does not match its visible text, voice control users cannot activate it using the words they see on screen. Saying "click Submit" to a button whose visual label is "Submit" but whose accessible name is "submitbtn" results in no action. This mismatch silently breaks voice control for citizens with motor disabilities who depend on this input method to interact with government websites.

Affected groups:

  • People who are blind using screen readers
  • People with low vision relying on screen magnifiers
  • People who are deaf using keyboard navigation
  • People with motor disabilities using voice control software

link-name: Link Purpose Clarity

Links without accessible names are completely useless to screen reader users who navigate government pages by jumping between links. An unnamed link could lead anywhere, and activating it unknowingly could trigger downloads, open unexpected pages, or initiate unintended processes. On government websites, unnamed links undermine the informed consent principle by preventing citizens from knowing where a link will take them.

Affected groups:

  • People who are blind using screen readers
  • People who are deaf and rely on visual-to-text tools
  • People with motor disabilities using keyboard navigation

image-alt: Visual Content Access

Images without alternative text are completely invisible to blind users and to search engines. On government websites, informational images, diagrams, infographics, and document screenshots without alt text deprive blind citizens of the same information available to sighted visitors. Critical details like emergency instructions, benefit eligibility charts, or form procedure diagrams can be entirely inaccessible, putting these citizens at a disadvantage in accessing government services and information.

Affected groups:

  • People who are blind using screen readers
  • People who are deaf and rely on visual-to-text tools

button-name: Button Discoverability

Unlabeled buttons are silent black holes in the government digital experience. When a screen reader announces a button with no name, blind users cannot know what action it will perform. Submit, cancel, search, and navigation buttons without accessible names prevent citizens from completing applications, accessing benefits, or finding essential government resources independently.

Affected groups:

  • People who are blind using screen readers
  • People who are deaf and rely on visual-to-text tools

aria-allowed-attr: Assistive Technology Reliability

Invalid ARIA attributes cause assistive technologies to misinterpret or malfunction on interactive controls. For screen reader users navigating complex government forms or applications, broken ARIA creates unpredictable behavior that can make tasks impossible to complete, denying access to essential services.

Affected groups:

  • People who are blind using screen readers
  • People who are deaf and rely on visual-to-text assistive tools
  • People with motor disabilities using switch access or voice control

link-in-text-block: Color-Independent Link Identification

When links are distinguished only by color, people with color vision deficiency cannot identify them as links and may miss critical references to additional information, forms, or resources. On government websites, overlooked links in informational content can mean missing essential instructions, deadlines, or procedures that affect a citizen's ability to access services or comply with requirements.

Affected groups:

  • People with low vision who may not perceive color differences
  • People who are color blind (approximately 8% of men, 0.5% of women)
Disability icon key
Without Vision — People who are blind or have no functional vision (1.0% of U.S. population — ~3,400,000 Americans)
Limited Vision — People with low vision who need magnification or high contrast (2.4% of U.S. population — ~8,100,000 Americans)
Without Perception of Color — People who cannot distinguish certain colors (color blindness) (4.3% of U.S. population — ~14,500,000 Americans)
Without Hearing — People who are deaf and cannot hear audio content (0.3% of U.S. population — ~1,100,000 Americans)
Limited Hearing — People with hearing loss who may struggle with audio without accommodations (3.5% of U.S. population — ~11,900,000 Americans)
Without Speech — People who cannot use speech or voice-based input effectively (0.5% of U.S. population — ~1,700,000 Americans)
Limited Manipulation — People with limited hand, finger, or fine motor dexterity (2.2% of U.S. population — ~7,600,000 Americans)
Limited Reach and Strength — People with limited reach, strength, or stamina (5.8% of U.S. population — ~19,600,000 Americans)
Limited Language, Cognitive, and Learning Abilities — People with cognitive, learning, or language differences (4.7% of U.S. population — ~15,900,000 Americans)

These icons show which groups of people with disabilities are excluded by each accessibility barrier. Where page view data is available, each icon shows an estimated number of people potentially excluded (page loads for affected URLs × disability prevalence rate from U.S. Census ACS 2023). Hover over or focus an icon to see the full estimate and methodology. Icons follow the Section 508 Functional Performance Criteria and the equivalent EU EN 301 549 v3.2.1 Table B.2 categories.

Prevalence data: U.S. Census Bureau, American Community Survey (ACS) 2023 1-Year Estimates, Table B18101; supplemental data from CDC, NIDCD, AFB, and NIH/NEI. U.S. population base: ~336,500,000 (2023 estimate). Reviewed annually.

See the Section 508 WCAG to FPC mapping for additional detail on how accessibility requirements map to functional needs.

Usability Heuristics Summary#

The most common accessibility violations map to Nielsen Norman Group's 10 usability heuristics. This table shows which heuristics are most affected by today's top axe-core violations, helping to identify where systemic usability barriers exist.

NN/g usability heuristics affected by today's top accessibility violations

8 Aesthetic and minimalist design 4 68 color-contrast, landmark-one-main, image-alt, link-in-text-block
10 Help and documentation 4 68 color-contrast, landmark-one-main, image-alt, link-in-text-block

1 Visibility of system status 3 57 color-contrast, landmark-one-main, link-in-text-block

Take Action#

Here is how you can help improve accessibility on U.S. government websites.

  • Read the federal accessibility report. The 2025 Governmentwide Section 508 Assessment from the GSA Administrator details the state of federal accessibility compliance and the steps agencies are taking to improve.
  • Submit URLs you care about. Want specific government pages included in future scans? Submit them to the Open Scans project to help broaden coverage.
  • Test with free tools. Do your own automated and manual accessibility testing with Accessibility Insights and Google Lighthouse. Automated tools catch a significant portion of WCAG failures quickly; manual review with assistive technologies catches the rest.
  • Adopt and update the U.S. Web Design System (USWDS). The USWDS is already a strong foundation for accessible, consistent federal websites, but many agencies have not adopted it or are running outdated versions. Encourage your agency to adopt or upgrade so every American benefits from its accessibility work.
  • Hire people with disabilities. People with disabilities are the ultimate experts on digital barriers. Bringing them into design, engineering, and testing teams — not just as consultants but as full-time employees — leads to more accessible products and services for everyone.

Accessibility Findings

https://tools.usps.com

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 68

Axe findings: 10

`button`, `link`, and `menuitem` elements do not have accessible names. (rule: aria-command-name)

When an element doesn't have an accessible name, screen readers announce it with a generic name, making it unusable for users who rely on screen readers. Learn how to make command elements more accessible.

Disabilities affected: ~63KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~63,766 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 6,376,589 affected page loads) ~19KPeople who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Estimated ~19,130 people potentially excluded (0.3% prevalence × 6,376,589 affected page loads) ~140KPeople with limited hand, finger, or fine motor dexterity. Estimated ~140,285 people potentially excluded (2.2% prevalence × 6,376,589 affected page loads)

Affected elements (3):

Element 1

Element path: div.panel-heading > h4.panel-title > a#headingOneAnchor > span.down-arr

Snippet:

<span role="button" data-parent="#accordion" aria-expanded="true" aria-controls="collapseOne" class="down-arr">

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Element does not have text that is visible to screen readers
  • aria-label attribute does not exist or is empty
  • aria-labelledby attribute does not exist, references elements that do not exist or references elements that are empty
  • Element has no title attribute

Element 2

Element path: div.panel-heading > h4.panel-title > a.collapsed > span.down-arr

Snippet:

<span role="button" data-parent="#accordion" aria-expanded="true" aria-controls="collapseOne" class="down-arr">

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Element does not have text that is visible to screen readers
  • aria-label attribute does not exist or is empty
  • aria-labelledby attribute does not exist, references elements that do not exist or references elements that are empty
  • Element has no title attribute

Element 3

Element path: div.panel-heading > h4.panel-title > a.collapsed > span.down-arr

Snippet:

<span role="button" data-parent="#accordion" aria-expanded="true" aria-controls="collapseOne" class="down-arr">

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Element does not have text that is visible to screen readers
  • aria-label attribute does not exist or is empty
  • aria-labelledby attribute does not exist, references elements that do not exist or references elements that are empty
  • Element has no title attribute
Elements with an ARIA `[role]` that require children to contain a specific `[role]` are missing some or all of those required children. (rule: aria-required-children)

Some ARIA parent roles must contain specific child roles to perform their intended accessibility functions. Learn more about roles and required children elements.

`[role]`s are not contained by their required parent element (rule: aria-required-parent)

Some ARIA child roles must be contained by specific parent roles to properly perform their intended accessibility functions. Learn more about ARIA roles and required parent element.

`[aria-*]` attributes do not have valid values (rule: aria-valid-attr-value)

Assistive technologies, like screen readers, can't interpret ARIA attributes with invalid values. Learn more about valid values for ARIA attributes.

`[aria-*]` attributes are not valid or misspelled (rule: aria-valid-attr)

Assistive technologies, like screen readers, can't interpret ARIA attributes with invalid names. Learn more about valid ARIA attributes.

Heading elements are not in a sequentially-descending order (rule: heading-order)

Properly ordered headings that do not skip levels convey the semantic structure of the page, making it easier to navigate and understand when using assistive technologies. Learn more about heading order.

Links do not have a discernible name (rule: link-name)

Link text (and alternate text for images, when used as links) that is discernible, unique, and focusable improves the navigation experience for screen reader users. Learn how to make links accessible.

List items (`<li>`) are not contained within `<ul>`, `<ol>` or `<menu>` parent elements. (rule: listitem)

Screen readers require list items (`<li>`) to be contained within a parent `<ul>`, `<ol>` or `<menu>` to be announced properly. Learn more about proper list structure.

One main landmark helps screen reader users navigate a web page. Learn more about landmarks.

Download full axe findings JSON | Download full axe findings CSV

Accessibility Findings

https://forecast.weather.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 61

Axe findings: 6

Background and foreground colors do not have a sufficient contrast ratio. (rule: color-contrast)

Low-contrast text is difficult or impossible for many users to read. Learn how to provide sufficient color contrast.

Disabilities affected: ~44KPeople with low vision who need magnification or high contrast. Estimated ~44,162 people potentially excluded (2.4% prevalence × 1,840,091 affected page loads) ~79KPeople who cannot distinguish certain colors (color blindness). Estimated ~79,124 people potentially excluded (4.3% prevalence × 1,840,091 affected page loads)

Affected elements (1):

Element 1

Element path: div.one-sixth-first > div#forecast-lookup > form#getForecast > input#inputstring

Snippet:

<input id="inputstring" name="inputstring" type="text" value="Enter location ..." onclick="this.value=''" autocomplete="off">

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Element has insufficient color contrast of 2.84 (foreground color: #999999, background color: #ffffff, font size: 8.0pt (10.6667px), font weight: normal). Expected contrast ratio of 4.5:1
`<html>` element does not have a `[lang]` attribute (rule: html-has-lang)

If a page doesn't specify a `lang` attribute, a screen reader assumes that the page is in the default language that the user chose when setting up the screen reader. If the page isn't actually in the default language, then the screen reader might not announce the page's text correctly. Learn more about the `lang` attribute.

Estimated ~86,484 people potentially excluded (4.7% prevalence × 1,840,091 affected page loads)

Element 1

Element path: html

Snippet:

<html xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1999/xhtml">

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • The <html> element does not have a lang attribute
Image elements do not have `[alt]` attributes (rule: image-alt)

Informative elements should aim for short, descriptive alternate text. Decorative elements can be ignored with an empty alt attribute. Learn more about the `alt` attribute.

Select elements do not have associated label elements. (rule: select-name)

Form elements without effective labels can create frustrating experiences for screen reader users. Learn more about the `select` element.

Element 1

Element path: div.warningArea > div#stateListings > form > select

Snippet:

<select size="1" name="menu">

Label: Warnings By State Warnings By County Alabama Alaska Arizona Arkansas California…

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Element does not have an implicit (wrapped) <label>
  • Element does not have an explicit <label>
  • aria-label attribute does not exist or is empty
  • aria-labelledby attribute does not exist, references elements that do not exist or references elements that are empty
  • Element has no title attribute
  • Element's default semantics were not overridden with role="none" or role="presentation"

Heading elements are not in a sequentially-descending order (rule: heading-order)

Properly ordered headings that do not skip levels convey the semantic structure of the page, making it easier to navigate and understand when using assistive technologies. Learn more about heading order.

https://usps.com

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 68

`[aria-*]` attributes do not match their roles (rule: aria-allowed-attr)

Each ARIA `role` supports a specific subset of `aria-*` attributes. Mismatching these invalidates the `aria-*` attributes. Learn how to match ARIA attributes to their roles.

Disabilities affected: ~9KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~9,497 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 949,749 affected page loads) ~2KPeople who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Image elements do not have `[alt]` attributes (rule: image-alt)

Informative elements should aim for short, descriptive alternate text. Decorative elements can be ignored with an empty alt attribute. Learn more about the `alt` attribute.

Links do not have a discernible name (rule: link-name)

Link text (and alternate text for images, when used as links) that is discernible, unique, and focusable improves the navigation experience for screen reader users. Learn how to make links accessible.

List items (`<li>`) are not contained within `<ul>`, `<ol>` or `<menu>` parent elements. (rule: listitem)

Screen readers require list items (`<li>`) to be contained within a parent `<ul>`, `<ol>` or `<menu>` to be announced properly. Learn more about proper list structure.

`[user-scalable="no"]` is used in the `<meta name="viewport">` element or the `[maximum-scale]` attribute is less than 5. (rule: meta-viewport)

Disabling zooming is problematic for users with low vision who rely on screen magnification to properly see the contents of a web page. Learn more about the viewport meta tag.

Disabilities affected: ~22KPeople with low vision who need magnification or high contrast. Estimated ~22,794 people potentially excluded (2.4% prevalence × 949,749 affected page loads)

Affected elements (1):

Element 1

Element path: head > meta

Snippet:

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • user-scalable=no on <meta> tag disables zooming on mobile devices
Touch targets do not have sufficient size or spacing. (rule: target-size)

Touch targets with sufficient size and spacing help users who may have difficulty targeting small controls to activate the targets. Learn more about touch targets.

  • Element has insufficient color contrast of 4.25 (foreground color: #337ab7, background color: #f7f7f7, font size: 12.0pt (16px), font weight: normal). Expected contrast ratio of 4.5:1
Heading elements are not in a sequentially-descending order (rule: heading-order)

Properly ordered headings that do not skip levels convey the semantic structure of the page, making it easier to navigate and understand when using assistive technologies. Learn more about heading order.

`<html>` element does not have a `[lang]` attribute (rule: html-has-lang)

If a page doesn't specify a `lang` attribute, a screen reader assumes that the page is in the default language that the user chose when setting up the screen reader. If the page isn't actually in the default language, then the screen reader might not announce the page's text correctly. Learn more about the `lang` attribute.

Links do not have a discernible name (rule: link-name)

Link text (and alternate text for images, when used as links) that is discernible, unique, and focusable improves the navigation experience for screen reader users. Learn how to make links accessible.

List items (`<li>`) are not contained within `<ul>`, `<ol>` or `<menu>` parent elements. (rule: listitem)

Screen readers require list items (`<li>`) to be contained within a parent `<ul>`, `<ol>` or `<menu>` to be announced properly. Learn more about proper list structure.

Image elements do not have `[alt]` attributes (rule: image-alt)

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Element does not have an alt attribute
  • aria-label attribute does not exist or is empty
  • aria-labelledby attribute does not exist, references elements that do not exist or references elements that are empty
  • Element has no title attribute
  • Element's default semantics were not overridden with role="none" or role="presentation"
Elements with visible text labels do not have matching accessible names. (rule: label-content-name-mismatch)

  • aria-labelledby attribute does not exist, references elements that do not exist or references elements that are empty
  • Element has no title attribute
  • Element's default semantics were not overridden with role="none" or role="presentation"
Elements with visible text labels do not have matching accessible names. (rule: label-content-name-mismatch)

Visible text labels that do not match the accessible name can result in a confusing experience for screen reader users. Learn more about accessible names.

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 97

Lists do not contain only `<li>` elements and script supporting elements (`<script>` and `<template>`). (rule: list)

Screen readers have a specific way of announcing lists. Ensuring proper list structure aids screen reader output. Learn more about proper list structure.

`<html>` element does not have a `[lang]` attribute (rule: html-has-lang)

If a page doesn't specify a `lang` attribute, a screen reader assumes that the page is in the default language that the user chose when setting up the screen reader. If the page isn't actually in the default language, then the screen reader might not announce the page's text correctly. Learn more about the `lang` attribute.

Accessibility Findings

https://travel.state.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 93

Axe findings: 2

`[user-scalable="no"]` is used in the `<meta name="viewport">` element or the `[maximum-scale]` attribute is less than 5. (rule: meta-viewport)

Disabling zooming is problematic for users with low vision who rely on screen magnification to properly see the contents of a web page. Learn more about the viewport meta tag.

Disabilities affected: ~13KPeople with low vision who need magnification or high contrast. Estimated ~13,773 people potentially excluded (2.4% prevalence × 573,865 affected page loads)

Affected elements (1):

Element 1

Element path: head > meta

Snippet:

<meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1.0, maximum-scale=1.0, user-scalable=no">

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • user-scalable=no on <meta> tag disables zooming on mobile devices

https://cdc.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 91

Axe findings: 3

Links do not have a discernible name (rule: link-name)

Link text (and alternate text for images, when used as links) that is discernible, unique, and focusable improves the navigation experience for screen reader users. Learn how to make links accessible.

Disabilities affected: ~5KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~5,681 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 568,057 affected page loads) ~1KPeople who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Estimated ~1,704 people potentially excluded (0.3% prevalence × 568,057 affected page loads) ~12KPeople with limited hand, finger, or fine motor dexterity. Estimated ~12,497 people potentially excluded (2.2% prevalence × 568,057 affected page loads)

Affected elements (1):

Element 1

Element path: nav.mobile-navbar > div.mobile-navbar_top > div.d-flex > a#cdc-mobile-search-toggle

Snippet:

<a id="cdc-mobile-search-toggle" class="nav-link" href="#" aria-controls="cdc-mobile-search-form">

How to fix: Fix all of the following:

  • Element is in tab order and does not have accessible text
  • Fix any of the following:
  • Element does not have text that is visible to screen readers
  • aria-label attribute does not exist or is empty
  • aria-labelledby attribute does not exist, references elements that do not exist or references elements that are empty
  • Element has no title attribute
Skip links are not focusable. (rule: skip-link)

Including a skip link can help users skip to the main content to save time. Learn more about skip links.

Element path: body.cdc-post-type--cdc-homepage > div#skipmenu > a.skippy

Label: Skip to search

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • No skip link target
Some elements have a `[tabindex]` value greater than 0 (rule: tabindex)

A value greater than 0 implies an explicit navigation ordering. Although technically valid, this often creates frustrating experiences for users who rely on assistive technologies. Learn more about the `tabindex` attribute.

Background and foreground colors do not have a sufficient contrast ratio. (rule: color-contrast)

Low-contrast text is difficult or impossible for many users to read. Learn how to provide sufficient color contrast.

Estimated ~9,536 people potentially excluded (2.4% prevalence × 397,320 affected page loads) ~17KPeople who cannot distinguish certain colors (color blindness). Heading elements are not in a sequentially-descending order (rule: heading-order)

Properly ordered headings that do not skip levels convey the semantic structure of the page, making it easier to navigate and understand when using assistive technologies. Learn more about heading order.

List items (`<li>`) are not contained within `<ul>`, `<ol>` or `<menu>` parent elements. (rule: listitem)

Axe findings: 2

`<html>` element does not have a `[lang]` attribute (rule: html-has-lang)

If a page doesn't specify a `lang` attribute, a screen reader assumes that the page is in the default language that the user chose when setting up the screen reader. If the page isn't actually in the default language, then the screen reader might not announce the page's text correctly. Learn more about the `lang` attribute.

Disabilities affected: ~3KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~3,175 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 317,502 affected page loads) ~953People who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Estimated ~953 people potentially excluded (0.3% prevalence × 317,502 affected page loads) ~14KPeople with cognitive, learning, or language differences. Estimated ~14,923 people potentially excluded (4.7% prevalence × 317,502 affected page loads)

Affected elements (1):

Element 1

Element path: html

Snippet:

<html>

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • The <html> element does not have a lang attribute
`[user-scalable="no"]` is used in the `<meta name="viewport">` element or the `[maximum-scale]` attribute is less than 5. (rule: meta-viewport)

Disabling zooming is problematic for users with low vision who rely on screen magnification to properly see the contents of a web page. Learn more about the viewport meta tag.

Disabilities affected: ~7KPeople with low vision who need magnification or high contrast. Estimated ~7,620 people potentially excluded (2.4% prevalence × 317,502 affected page loads)

Affected elements (1):

Element 1

Element path: head > meta

Snippet:

<meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1, maximum-scale=1, user-scalable=0">

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • user-scalable on <meta> tag disables zooming on mobile devices

Accessibility Findings

https://fas.usda.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 97

Axe findings: 2

`[aria-hidden="true"]` elements contain focusable descendents (rule: aria-hidden-focus)

Focusable descendents within an `[aria-hidden="true"]` element prevent those interactive elements from being available to users of assistive technologies like screen readers. Learn how `aria-hidden` affects focusable elements.

Estimated ~6,952 people potentially excluded (2.2% prevalence × 316,007 affected page loads)

Elements with visible text labels do not have matching accessible names. (rule: label-content-name-mismatch)

Visible text labels that do not match the accessible name can result in a confusing experience for screen reader users. Learn more about accessible names.

Accessibility Findings

https://science.nasa.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 95

Axe findings: 1

Buttons do not have an accessible name (rule: button-name)

When a button doesn't have an accessible name, screen readers announce it as "button", making it unusable for users who rely on screen readers. Learn how to make buttons more accessible.

Disabilities affected: ~3KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~3,049 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 304,939 affected page loads) ~915People who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Estimated ~915 people potentially excluded (0.3% prevalence × 304,939 affected page loads)

Affected elements (4):

Element 1

Element path: header.hds-nasa-header > div.usa-nav-container > div.grid-col > button#global-navigation-trigger--mobile

Snippet:

<button id="global-navigation-trigger--mobile" class="hds-global-menu-toggle menu-toggle menu-mobile-toggle" aria-expanded="false" data-menu-action="open" data-menu-location="left">

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Element does not have inner text that is visible to screen readers
  • aria-label attribute does not exist or is empty
  • aria-labelledby attribute does not exist, references elements that do not exist or references elements that are empty
  • Element has no title attribute
  • Element does not have an implicit (wrapped) <label>
  • Element does not have an explicit <label>
  • Element's default semantics were not overridden with role="none" or role="presentation"

  • aria-labelledby attribute does not exist, references elements that do not exist or references elements that are empty
  • Element has no title attribute
  • Element does not have an implicit (wrapped) <label>
  • Element does not have an explicit <label>
  • Element's default semantics were not overridden with role="none" or role="presentation"

  • Element does not have an implicit (wrapped) <label>

Accessibility Findings

https://usajobs.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 98

Axe findings: 1

Document does not have a main landmark. (rule: landmark-one-main)

One main landmark helps screen reader users navigate a web page. Learn more about landmarks.

Estimated ~765 people potentially excluded (0.3% prevalence × 255,150 affected page loads) ~5KPeople with limited hand, finger, or fine motor dexterity. Estimated ~5,613 people potentially excluded (2.2% prevalence × 255,150 affected page loads)

Element 1

Element path: html.flexbox

Snippet:

<html lang="en" class="flexbox scroll-smooth">

  • Document does not have a main landmark

Accessibility Findings

https://esta.cbp.dhs.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 85

Axe findings: 4

Image elements do not have `[alt]` attributes (rule: image-alt)

Informative elements should aim for short, descriptive alternate text. Decorative elements can be ignored with an empty alt attribute. Learn more about the `alt` attribute.

Disabilities affected: ~2KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~2,533 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 253,287 affected page loads) ~760People who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Estimated ~760 people potentially excluded (0.3% prevalence × 253,287 affected page loads)

Affected elements (1):

Element 1

Element path: div.toggle-language > span.dropdown > button#languageDrop > img

Snippet:

<img _ngcontent-ng-c799267185="" src="assets/images/flags/usUkFlag.png">

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Element does not have an alt attribute
  • aria-label attribute does not exist or is empty
  • aria-labelledby attribute does not exist, references elements that do not exist or references elements that are empty
  • Element has no title attribute
  • Element's default semantics were not overridden with role="none" or role="presentation"
Links rely on color to be distinguishable. (rule: link-in-text-block)

Low-contrast text is difficult or impossible for many users to read. Link text that is discernible improves the experience for users with low vision. Learn how to make links distinguishable.

Disabilities affected: ~6KPeople with low vision who need magnification or high contrast. Estimated ~6,079 people potentially excluded (2.4% prevalence × 253,287 affected page loads) ~10KPeople who cannot distinguish certain colors (color blindness). Estimated ~10,891 people potentially excluded (4.3% prevalence × 253,287 affected page loads)

Affected elements (1):

Element 1

Element path: div.col-xs-12 > ul > li#welcome.whoYouAre.1 > a.strong

Snippet:

<a tabindex="0" data-target="#visaModal" data-toggle="modal" class="strong esta-link" href="#">

Label: Visa Waiver Program

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • The link has insufficient color contrast of 1.18:1 with the surrounding text. (Minimum contrast is 3:1, link text: #00416a, surrounding text: #333333)
  • The link has no styling (such as underline) to distinguish it from the surrounding text
Lists do not contain only `<li>` elements and script supporting elements (`<script>` and `<template>`). (rule: list)

Screen readers have a specific way of announcing lists. Ensuring proper list structure aids screen reader output. Learn more about proper list structure.

Disabilities affected: ~2KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~2,533 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 253,287 affected page loads) ~760People who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Estimated ~760 people potentially excluded (0.3% prevalence × 253,287 affected page loads)

Element 1

Element path: div.container > div.row > div.col-xs-12 > ul

Snippet:

<ul _ngcontent-ng-c2940030545="">

Label: About ESTA and The Visa Waiver Program What is the difference between an ESTA …

How to fix: Fix all of the following:

  • List element has direct children that are not allowed: div
List items (`<li>`) are not contained within `<ul>`, `<ol>` or `<menu>` parent elements. (rule: listitem)

Screen readers require list items (`<li>`) to be contained within a parent `<ul>`, `<ol>` or `<menu>` to be announced properly. Learn more about proper list structure.

https://reg.usps.com

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 97

Axe findings: 1

Touch targets do not have sufficient size or spacing. (rule: target-size)

Touch targets with sufficient size and spacing help users who may have difficulty targeting small controls to activate the targets. Learn more about touch targets.

Estimated ~4,811 people potentially excluded (2.2% prevalence × 218,695 affected page loads) ~12KPeople with limited reach, strength, or stamina. Estimated ~12,684 people potentially excluded (5.8% prevalence × 218,695 affected page loads)

Affected elements (15):

Element 1

Element path: div.row > div.col-xs-12 > sup > a#password-tip

Snippet:

<a role="button" id="password-tip" tabindex="0" data-placement="top" data-remotecontent="/entreg/assets/html/tooltips/tool-tip-forgot-useralias.html" data-toggle="popover" aria-label="Retrieve your username" data-bs-original-title="Retrieve your username">

Label: Retrieve your username

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Target has insufficient size (12px by 15px, should be at least 24px by 24px)
  • Target has insufficient space to its closest neighbors. Safe clickable space has a diameter of 21px instead of at least 24px.

  • Target has insufficient space to its closest neighbors. Safe clickable space has a diameter of 16.4px instead of at least 24px.

Element 15

Element path: div.col-6 > ul.list-unstyled > li > a

Snippet:

<a href="https://about.usps.com/who/legal/terms-of-use.htm" tabindex="0">

Label: Terms of Use

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Target has insufficient size (66.5px by 13px, should be at least 24px by 24px)
  • Target has insufficient space to its closest neighbors. Safe clickable space has a diameter of 16.4px instead of at least 24px.

Accessibility Findings

https://urs.earthdata.nasa.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 81

Axe findings: 5

Buttons do not have an accessible name (rule: button-name)

When a button doesn't have an accessible name, screen readers announce it as "button", making it unusable for users who rely on screen readers. Learn how to make buttons more accessible.

Disabilities affected: ~2KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~2,136 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 213,576 affected page loads) ~641People who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Estimated ~641 people potentially excluded (0.3% prevalence × 213,576 affected page loads)

Affected elements (1):

Element 1

Element path: div#tophat3Widget > nav#tophat3WidgetNavBar > div.container-fluid > button#tophat3HamburgerButton

Snippet:

<button class="navbar-toggler" type="button" id="tophat3HamburgerButton">

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Element does not have inner text that is visible to screen readers
  • aria-label attribute does not exist or is empty
  • aria-labelledby attribute does not exist, references elements that do not exist or references elements that are empty
  • Element has no title attribute
  • Element does not have an implicit (wrapped) <label>
  • Element does not have an explicit <label>
  • Element's default semantics were not overridden with role="none" or role="presentation"
Background and foreground colors do not have a sufficient contrast ratio. (rule: color-contrast)

Low-contrast text is difficult or impossible for many users to read. Learn how to provide sufficient color contrast.

Disabilities affected: ~5KPeople with low vision who need magnification or high contrast. Estimated ~5,126 people potentially excluded (2.4% prevalence × 213,576 affected page loads) ~9KPeople who cannot distinguish certain colors (color blindness). Estimated ~9,184 people potentially excluded (4.3% prevalence × 213,576 affected page loads)

  • Element has insufficient color contrast of 2.07 (foreground color: #ffffff, background color: #20ce6f, font size: 12.0pt (16px), font weight: normal). Expected contrast ratio of 4.5:1
Image elements do not have `[alt]` attributes (rule: image-alt)

Informative elements should aim for short, descriptive alternate text. Decorative elements can be ignored with an empty alt attribute. Learn more about the `alt` attribute.

Links rely on color to be distinguishable. (rule: link-in-text-block)

Low-contrast text is difficult or impossible for many users to read. Link text that is discernible improves the experience for users with low vision. Learn how to make links distinguishable.

  • Element's default semantics were not overridden with role="none" or role="presentation"
`<html>` element does not have a `[lang]` attribute (rule: html-has-lang)

If a page doesn't specify a `lang` attribute, a screen reader assumes that the page is in the default language that the user chose when setting up the screen reader. If the page isn't actually in the default language, then the screen reader might not announce the page's text correctly. Learn more about the `lang` attribute.

Snippet:

<html xmlns="//www.w3.org/1999/xhtml">

  • The <html> element does not have a lang attribute
Image elements do not have `[alt]` attributes (rule: image-alt)

Informative elements should aim for short, descriptive alternate text. Decorative elements can be ignored with an empty alt attribute. Learn more about the `alt` attribute.

Links do not have a discernible name (rule: link-name)

Link text (and alternate text for images, when used as links) that is discernible, unique, and focusable improves the navigation experience for screen reader users. Learn how to make links accessible.

  • Target has insufficient space to its closest neighbors. Safe clickable space has a diameter of 4px instead of at least 24px.

Document does not have a main landmark. (rule: landmark-one-main)

One main landmark helps screen reader users navigate a web page. Learn more about landmarks.

Disabilities affected: ~1KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~1,716 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 171,625 affected page loads) ~515People who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Estimated ~515 people potentially excluded (0.3% prevalence × 171,625 affected page loads) ~3KPeople with limited hand, finger, or fine motor dexterity. Estimated ~3,776 people potentially excluded (2.2% prevalence × 171,625 affected page loads)

Affected elements (1):

Element 1

Element path: html

Snippet:

<html xmlns="//www.w3.org/1999/xhtml">

How to fix: Fix all of the following:

  • Document does not have a main landmark
`<td>` elements in a large `<table>` do not have table headers. (rule: td-has-header)

Screen readers have features to make navigating tables easier. Ensuring that `<td>` elements in a large table (3 or more cells in width and height) have an associated table header may improve the experience for screen reader users. Learn more about table headers.

Disabilities affected: ~1KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~1,716 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 171,625 affected page loads) ~515People who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Estimated ~515 people potentially excluded (0.3% prevalence × 171,625 affected page loads)

Affected elements (1):

Element 1

Element path: tbody > tr > td > table

Snippet:

<table cellspacing="1" cellpadding="2" border="0" width="100%">

Label: Hazard Tue (03/10) Wed (03/11) Thu (03/12) Fri (03/13) Sat (03/14) Sun (03/15) …

How to fix: Fix all of the following:

  • Some non-empty data cells do not have table headers

Download full axe findings JSON | Download full axe findings CSV

Accessibility Findings

https://tsp.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 91

Axe findings: 2

Buttons do not have an accessible name (rule: button-name)

When a button doesn't have an accessible name, screen readers announce it as "button", making it unusable for users who rely on screen readers. Learn how to make buttons more accessible.

Disabilities affected: ~1KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~1,648 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 164,826 affected page loads) ~494People who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Estimated ~494 people potentially excluded (0.3% prevalence × 164,826 affected page loads)

Affected elements (1):

Element 1

Element path: aside#tsp-toolbar > ul > li > button#toggle-tsp-zoom

Snippet:

<button id="toggle-tsp-zoom" class="tooltip">

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Element does not have inner text that is visible to screen readers
  • aria-label attribute does not exist or is empty
  • aria-labelledby attribute does not exist, references elements that do not exist or references elements that are empty
  • Element has no title attribute
  • Element does not have an implicit (wrapped) <label>
  • Element does not have an explicit <label>
  • Element's default semantics were not overridden with role="none" or role="presentation"
Links rely on color to be distinguishable. (rule: link-in-text-block)

Low-contrast text is difficult or impossible for many users to read. Link text that is discernible improves the experience for users with low vision. Learn how to make links distinguishable.

Disabilities affected: ~3KPeople with low vision who need magnification or high contrast. Estimated ~3,956 people potentially excluded (2.4% prevalence × 164,826 affected page loads) ~7KPeople who cannot distinguish certain colors (color blindness). Estimated ~7,088 people potentially excluded (4.3% prevalence × 164,826 affected page loads)

Affected elements (1):

Element 1

Element path: div.usa-grid > div.usa-footer-logo > p > a

Snippet:

<a href="https://www.tsp.gov/contact/">

Label: contact us

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • The link has insufficient color contrast of 1.5:1 with the surrounding text. (Minimum contrast is 3:1, link text: #d6d7d9, surrounding text: #aeb0b5)
  • The link has no styling (such as underline) to distinguish it from the surrounding text

Download full axe findings JSON | Download full axe findings CSV

Accessibility Findings

https://usa.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 98

Axe findings: 1

Heading elements are not in a sequentially-descending order (rule: heading-order)

Properly ordered headings that do not skip levels convey the semantic structure of the page, making it easier to navigate and understand when using assistive technologies. Learn more about heading order.

Disabilities affected: ~1KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~1,640 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 164,048 affected page loads) ~492People who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Estimated ~492 people potentially excluded (0.3% prevalence × 164,048 affected page loads) ~3KPeople with limited hand, finger, or fine motor dexterity. Estimated ~3,609 people potentially excluded (2.2% prevalence × 164,048 affected page loads)

Affected elements (1):

Element 1

Element path: div > div#footer-phone > div.mobile-lg:grid-col-auto > h4.usa-footer__logo-heading

Snippet:

<h4 class="usa-footer__logo-heading">

Label: USAGov Contact Center

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Heading order invalid

Download full axe findings JSON | Download full axe findings CSV

Accessibility Findings

https://ecfr.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 73

Axe findings: 7

`[aria-*]` attributes do not match their roles (rule: aria-allowed-attr)

Each ARIA `role` supports a specific subset of `aria-*` attributes. Mismatching these invalidates the `aria-*` attributes. Learn how to match ARIA attributes to their roles.

Disabilities affected: ~1KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~1,603 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 160,282 affected page loads) ~481People who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Estimated ~481 people potentially excluded (0.3% prevalence × 160,282 affected page loads) ~3KPeople with limited hand, finger, or fine motor dexterity. Estimated ~3,526 people potentially excluded (2.2% prevalence × 160,282 affected page loads)

Affected elements (1):

Element 1

Element path: div.row > div.col-xs-12 > div > div#suggestions

Snippet:

<div id="suggestions" data-controller="suggestions autocomplete" data-autocomplete-url-value="/suggestions" data-autocomplete-delay-value="300" aria-expanded="false" data-autocomplete-ready-value="true">

Label:

How to fix: Fix all of the following:

  • ARIA attribute is not allowed: aria-expanded="false"
Buttons do not have an accessible name (rule: button-name)

When a button doesn't have an accessible name, screen readers announce it as "button", making it unusable for users who rely on screen readers. Learn how to make buttons more accessible.

Disabilities affected: ~1KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~1,603 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 160,282 affected page loads) ~481People who are deaf and cannot hear audio content.   • Element does not have inner text that is visible to screen readers
  • aria-label attribute does not exist or is empty
  • aria-labelledby attribute does not exist, references elements that do not exist or references elements that are empty
  • Element has no title attribute
  • Element does not have an implicit (wrapped) <label>
  • Element does not have an explicit <label>
  • Element's default semantics were not overridden with role="none" or role="presentation"
Background and foreground colors do not have a sufficient contrast ratio. (rule: color-contrast)

Low-contrast text is difficult or impossible for many users to read. Learn how to provide sufficient color contrast.

Disabilities affected: ~3KPeople with low vision who need magnification or high contrast. Estimated ~3,847 people potentially excluded (2.4% prevalence × 160,282 affected page loads) ~6KPeople who cannot distinguish certain colors (color blindness). Estimated ~6,892 people potentially excluded (4.3% prevalence × 160,282 affected page loads)

Links rely on color to be distinguishable. (rule: link-in-text-block)

Low-contrast text is difficult or impossible for many users to read. Link text that is discernible improves the experience for users with low vision. Learn how to make links distinguishable.

Disabilities affected: ~3KPeople with low vision who need magnification or high contrast. ARIA input fields do not have accessible names (rule: aria-input-field-name)

When an input field doesn't have an accessible name, screen readers announce it with a generic name, making it unusable for users who rely on screen readers. Learn more about input field labels.

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 89

Axe findings: 2

`[aria-*]` attributes do not match their roles (rule: aria-allowed-attr)

Each ARIA `role` supports a specific subset of `aria-*` attributes. Mismatching these invalidates the `aria-*` attributes. Learn how to match ARIA attributes to their roles.

`[user-scalable="no"]` is used in the `<meta name="viewport">` element or the `[maximum-scale]` attribute is less than 5. (rule: meta-viewport)

Disabling zooming is problematic for users with low vision who rely on screen magnification to properly see the contents of a web page. Learn more about the viewport meta tag.

Disabilities affected: ~3KPeople with low vision who need magnification or high contrast. Estimated ~3,355 people potentially excluded (2.4% prevalence × 139,798 affected page loads)

Element path: head > meta

Snippet:

<meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width,height=device-height,user-scalable=no,initial-scale=1.0…">

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • user-scalable=no on <meta> tag disables zooming on mobile devices

Elements with visible text labels do not have matching accessible names. (rule: label-content-name-mismatch)

Visible text labels that do not match the accessible name can result in a confusing experience for screen reader users. Learn more about accessible names.

Heading elements are not in a sequentially-descending order (rule: heading-order)

Properly ordered headings that do not skip levels convey the semantic structure of the page, making it easier to navigate and understand when using assistive technologies. Learn more about heading order.

  • Heading order invalid
`<html>` element does not have a `[lang]` attribute (rule: html-has-lang)

If a page doesn't specify a `lang` attribute, a screen reader assumes that the page is in the default language that the user chose when setting up the screen reader. If the page isn't actually in the default language, then the screen reader might not announce the page's text correctly. Learn more about the `lang` attribute.

Form elements do not have associated labels (rule: label)

Labels ensure that form controls are announced properly by assistive technologies, like screen readers. Learn more about form element labels.

  • aria-label attribute does not exist or is empty

  • Element has no title attribute
  • Element has no placeholder attribute
  • Element's default semantics were not overridden with role="none" or role="presentation"

Touch targets do not have sufficient size or spacing. (rule: target-size)

Touch targets with sufficient size and spacing help users who may have difficulty targeting small controls to activate the targets. Learn more about touch targets.

Links rely on color to be distinguishable. (rule: link-in-text-block)

Low-contrast text is difficult or impossible for many users to read. Link text that is discernible improves the experience for users with low vision. Learn how to make links distinguishable.

Disabilities affected: ~2KPeople with low vision who need magnification or high contrast. Estimated ~2,864 people potentially excluded (2.4% prevalence × 119,349 affected page loads) ~5KPeople who cannot distinguish certain colors (color blindness). Estimated ~5,132 people potentially excluded (4.3% prevalence × 119,349 affected page loads)

Affected elements (2):

Element 1

Element path: div#content > div.toolbar > div.search-helper > a

Snippet:

<a href="https://docs.ckan.org/en/2.10/user-guide.html?#search-in-detail" target="_blank">

Label: "Search in Detail" instructions.

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • The link has insufficient color contrast of 1.92:1 with the surrounding text. (Minimum contrast is 3:1, link text: #284a7a, surrounding text: #1b1b1b)
  • The link has no styling (such as underline) to distinguish it from the surrounding text

Element 2

Element path: div.dataset-content > div.notes > div > a

Snippet:

<a href="http://on.ny.gov/1GpWiHD" rel="noopener">

Label: http://on.ny.gov/1GpWiHD

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • The link has insufficient color contrast of 1.92:1 with the surrounding text. (Minimum contrast is 3:1, link text: #284a7a, surrounding text: #1b1b1b)
  • The link has no styling (such as underline) to distinguish it from the surrounding text
Links do not have a discernible name (rule: link-name)

Link text (and alternate text for images, when used as links) that is discernible, unique, and focusable improves the navigation experience for screen reader users. Learn how to make links accessible.

Snippet:

<a href="/dataset/">

How to fix: Fix all of the following:

  • Fix any of the following:

  • aria-labelledby attribute does not exist, references elements that do not exist or references elements that are empty
  • Element has no title attribute
Lists do not contain only `<li>` elements and script supporting elements (`<script>` and `<template>`). (rule: list)

Screen readers have a specific way of announcing lists. Ensuring proper list structure aids screen reader output. Learn more about proper list structure.

List items (`<li>`) are not contained within `<ul>`, `<ol>` or `<menu>` parent elements. (rule: listitem)

Screen readers require list items (`<li>`) to be contained within a parent `<ul>`, `<ol>` or `<menu>` to be announced properly. Learn more about proper list structure.

https://ncei.noaa.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 98

Axe findings: 2

Document does not have a main landmark. (rule: landmark-one-main)

One main landmark helps screen reader users navigate a web page. Learn more about landmarks.

Disabilities affected: ~1KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~1,153 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 115,267 affected page loads) ~346People who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Estimated ~346 people potentially excluded (0.3% prevalence × 115,267 affected page loads) ~2KPeople with limited hand, finger, or fine motor dexterity. Estimated ~2,536 people potentially excluded (2.2% prevalence × 115,267 affected page loads)

Accessibility Findings

https://fdic.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 82

Axe findings: 5

`[aria-*]` attributes do not match their roles (rule: aria-allowed-attr)

Each ARIA `role` supports a specific subset of `aria-*` attributes. Mismatching these invalidates the `aria-*` attributes. Learn how to match ARIA attributes to their roles.

Disabilities affected: ~1KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~1,118 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 111,788 affected page loads) ~335People who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Estimated ~335 people potentially excluded (0.3% prevalence × 111,788 affected page loads) ~2KPeople with limited hand, finger, or fine motor dexterity. Estimated ~2,459 people potentially excluded (2.2% prevalence × 111,788 affected page loads)

Affected elements (3):

Element 1

Element path: div.field__item > div.paragraph > div.tabs-container > input#consumers

Snippet:

<input class="radiotab" name="tabs" type="radio" id="consumers" checked="checked" aria-expanded="false" data-once="tab-click">

How to fix: Fix all of the following:

  • ARIA attribute is not allowed: aria-expanded="false"

Element 2

Element path: div.field__item > div.paragraph > div.tabs-container > input#bankers

Snippet:

<input class="radiotab" name="tabs" type="radio" id="bankers" aria-expanded="false" data-once="tab-click">

Element 3

Element path: div.field__item > div.paragraph > div.tabs-container > input#analysts

Snippet:

<input class="radiotab" name="tabs" type="radio" id="analysts" aria-expanded="false" data-once="tab-click">

How to fix: Fix all of the following:

  • ARIA attribute is not allowed: aria-expanded="false"
Buttons do not have an accessible name (rule: button-name)

When a button doesn't have an accessible name, screen readers announce it as "button", making it unusable for users who rely on screen readers. Learn how to make buttons more accessible.

Disabilities affected: ~1KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~1,118 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 111,788 affected page loads) ~335People who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Touch targets do not have sufficient size or spacing. (rule: target-size)

Affected elements (3):

Element 1

Element path: div.margin-y-1 > div.fs-site-branding > div.display-flex > a.font-body-md

Snippet:

<a class="font-body-md text-no-underline text-base-darker" href="https://www.usda.gov/" data-kmt="1">

Label: U.S.D.A

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Target has insufficient size (152.8px by 20.3px, should be at least 24px by 24px)

Heading elements are not in a sequentially-descending order (rule: heading-order)

Some elements have a `[tabindex]` value greater than 0 (rule: tabindex)

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 87

Axe findings: 3

Buttons do not have an accessible name (rule: button-name)

When a button doesn't have an accessible name, screen readers announce it as "button", making it unusable for users who rely on screen readers. Learn how to make buttons more accessible.

Estimated ~1,000 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 100,006 affected page loads) ~300People who are deaf and cannot hear audio content.   • Element does not have inner text that is visible to screen readers
  • aria-label attribute does not exist or is empty
  • aria-labelledby attribute does not exist, references elements that do not exist or references elements that are empty

  • Element does not have an implicit (wrapped) <label>
  • Element does not have an explicit <label>
  • Element's default semantics were not overridden with role="none" or role="presentation"

Links do not have a discernible name (rule: link-name)

Link text (and alternate text for images, when used as links) that is discernible, unique, and focusable improves the navigation experience for screen reader users. Learn how to make links accessible.

List items (`<li>`) are not contained within `<ul>`, `<ol>` or `<menu>` parent elements. (rule: listitem)

Screen readers require list items (`<li>`) to be contained within a parent `<ul>`, `<ol>` or `<menu>` to be announced properly. Learn more about proper list structure.

Heading elements are not in a sequentially-descending order (rule: heading-order)

Properly ordered headings that do not skip levels convey the semantic structure of the page, making it easier to navigate and understand when using assistive technologies. Learn more about heading order.

Accessibility Findings

https://ttp.cbp.dhs.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 90

Axe findings: 3

`[aria-*]` attributes do not match their roles (rule: aria-allowed-attr)

Each ARIA `role` supports a specific subset of `aria-*` attributes. Mismatching these invalidates the `aria-*` attributes. Learn how to match ARIA attributes to their roles.

Element 1

Element path: div.nav-row > div.container > nav.navbar > a.navbar-toggle

Snippet:

Label: MENU

Each ARIA `role` supports a specific subset of `aria-*` attributes. Mismatching these invalidates the `aria-*` attributes. Learn how to match ARIA attributes to their roles.

ARIA input fields do not have accessible names (rule: aria-input-field-name)

When an input field doesn't have an accessible name, screen readers announce it with a generic name, making it unusable for users who rely on screen readers. Learn more about input field labels.

Accessibility Findings

https://careers.cbp.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 75

Axe findings: 7

Elements with `role="dialog"` or `role="alertdialog"` do not have accessible names. (rule: aria-dialog-name)

ARIA dialog elements without accessible names may prevent screen readers users from discerning the purpose of these elements. Learn how to make ARIA dialog elements more accessible.

Affected elements (1):

Element 1

Element path: div > div.ui-widget > c-crs-custom-modal > section.slds-modal

Snippet:

<section lwc-6m11k7ek9iv="" class="slds-modal slds-fade-in-open" role="dialog" tabindex="-1" aria-model="true" aria-labelledby="modal-heading-01-17">

Label: GET UP TO $60,000 IN INCENTIVES WHEN YOU JOIN CBP LET'S GO

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • aria-label attribute does not exist or is empty
  • aria-labelledby attribute does not exist, references elements that do not exist or references elements that are empty
  • Element has no title attribute
Elements use prohibited ARIA attributes (rule: aria-prohibited-attr)

Using ARIA attributes in roles where they are prohibited can mean that important information is not communicated to users of assistive technologies. Learn more about prohibited ARIA roles.

Affected elements (2):

Element 1

Element path: div > span.inline-text > span.inline-text > lightning-combobox.super-icon-override

Snippet:

Label: Select an Option

How to fix: Fix all of the following:

  • aria-label attribute cannot be used on a lightning-combobox with no valid role attribute.

Element path: div.slds-align_absolute-center > div > span.inline-text > lightning-combobox.super-icon-override

Snippet:

<lightning-combobox lwc-60i0q72qjve="" class="super-icon-override slds-form-element" aria-label="Select Program Office" lwc-24on3e8rogg-host="" style="display: flex;">

Label: Select an Option

How to fix: Fix all of the following:

  • aria-label attribute cannot be used on a lightning-combobox with no valid role attribute.
`[aria-*]` attributes are not valid or misspelled (rule: aria-valid-attr)

Assistive technologies, like screen readers, can't interpret ARIA attributes with invalid names. Learn more about valid ARIA attributes.

Buttons do not have an accessible name (rule: button-name)

When a button doesn't have an accessible name, screen readers announce it as "button", making it unusable for users who rely on screen readers. Learn how to make buttons more accessible.

Image elements do not have `[alt]` attributes (rule: image-alt)

`<td>` elements in a large `<table>` do not have table headers. (rule: td-has-header)

Screen readers have features to make navigating tables easier. Ensuring that `<td>` elements in a large table (3 or more cells in width and height) have an associated table header may improve the experience for screen reader users. Learn more about table headers.

Heading elements are not in a sequentially-descending order (rule: heading-order)

Properly ordered headings that do not skip levels convey the semantic structure of the page, making it easier to navigate and understand when using assistive technologies. Learn more about heading order.

`[user-scalable="no"]` is used in the `<meta name="viewport">` element or the `[maximum-scale]` attribute is less than 5. (rule: meta-viewport)

Disabling zooming is problematic for users with low vision who rely on screen magnification to properly see the contents of a web page. Learn more about the viewport meta tag.

Accessibility Findings

https://water.noaa.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 88

Axe findings: 4

Buttons do not have an accessible name (rule: button-name)

When a button doesn't have an accessible name, screen readers announce it as "button", making it unusable for users who rely on screen readers. Learn how to make buttons more accessible.

Disabilities affected: ~776People who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~776 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 77,624 affected page loads) ~233People who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Estimated ~233 people potentially excluded (0.3% prevalence × 77,624 affected page loads)

Affected elements (1):

Element 1

Element path: div#nav-app > nav#navbar-container > div.uk-navbar-right > button.toggle-button

Snippet:

<button class="toggle-button svelte-1t22yhi uk-icon" uk-icon="icon: menu; ratio: 1.5">

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Element does not have inner text that is visible to screen readers
  • aria-label attribute does not exist or is empty
  • aria-labelledby attribute does not exist, references elements that do not exist or references elements that are empty
  • Element has no title attribute
  • Element does not have an implicit (wrapped) <label>
  • Element does not have an explicit <label>
  • Element's default semantics were not overridden with role="none" or role="presentation"
Background and foreground colors do not have a sufficient contrast ratio. (rule: color-contrast)

Low-contrast text is difficult or impossible for many users to read. Learn how to provide sufficient color contrast.

Disabilities affected: ~1KPeople with low vision who need magnification or high contrast. Estimated ~1,863 people potentially excluded (2.4% prevalence × 77,624 affected page loads) ~3KPeople who cannot distinguish certain colors (color blindness). Estimated ~3,338 people potentially excluded (4.3% prevalence × 77,624 affected page loads)

Affected elements (2):

Element 1

Element path: div.uk-grid > div.first-two-links > div.title-container > p.svelte-15brdhb

Snippet:

<p class="svelte-15brdhb">

Label: EXPERIMENTAL

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Element has insufficient color contrast of 1.97 (foreground color: #ffa500, background color: #ffffff, font size: 12.0pt (16px), font weight: bold). Expected contrast ratio of 4.5:1

Element 2

Element path: div.uk-grid > div > div.title-container > p.svelte-15brdhb

Snippet:

<p class="svelte-15brdhb">

Label: EXPERIMENTAL

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Element has insufficient color contrast of 1.97 (foreground color: #ffa500, background color: #ffffff, font size: 12.0pt (16px), font weight: bold). Expected contrast ratio of 4.5:1
Touch targets do not have sufficient size or spacing. (rule: target-size)

Touch targets with sufficient size and spacing help users who may have difficulty targeting small controls to activate the targets. Learn more about touch targets.

Disabilities affected: ~1KPeople with limited hand, finger, or fine motor dexterity. Estimated ~1,708 people potentially excluded (2.2% prevalence × 77,624 affected page loads) ~4KPeople with limited reach, strength, or stamina. Estimated ~4,502 people potentially excluded (5.8% prevalence × 77,624 affected page loads)

Affected elements (11):

Element 1

Element path: div#map-app > div#controls > div.uk-flex > button

Snippet:

<button style="border: 'none'; 'background': none; padding: 0;">

Label: View Layers

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Target has insufficient size because it is partially obscured (smallest space is 33px by 4px, should be at least 24px by 24px)
  • Target has insufficient space to its closest neighbors. Safe clickable space has a diameter of 0px instead of at least 24px.

Elements with visible text labels do not have matching accessible names. (rule: label-content-name-mismatch)

Visible text labels that do not match the accessible name can result in a confusing experience for screen reader users. Learn more about accessible names.

https://state.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 97

Axe findings: 1

Links do not have a discernible name (rule: link-name)

Link text (and alternate text for images, when used as links) that is discernible, unique, and focusable improves the navigation experience for screen reader users. Learn how to make links accessible.
